coredata.dc:title: Surface states engineering carbon dots as multi-band light active sensitizers for ZnO nanowire array photoanode to boost solar water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaoyong
Bao, Zhijia
Tang, Wenshuai
Wu, Haiyan
Pan, Jing
Hu, Jingguo
Zeng, Haibo
coredata.dc:description: Efficient capture of solar photons is crucial in photo-electrochemical (PEC) water splitting devices for converting solar energy to hydrogen as an energy-dense carrier. Herein, we first reported the unique multi-band light absorption character on carbon dots (CDs) with controllable surface states and built an efficient photoanode by bonding such CDs as favorable solar photosensitizers on one-dimensional (1D) ZnO nanorod arrays (NRAs) for the PEC solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion. The multi-transition models related to surface CO, and COH states with different energy levels were identified to dominate the CDs' multi-model optical absorption covering the full-range visible region in the solar spectrum, which renders an excellent advantage of CDs in serving as the solar photosensitizer for photoelectric systems. Moreover, the fabricated ZnO@CDs heterostructure photoanode with the functionalized CDs used to harvest solar photons along with subsequent charge separation at heterointerface and transport along 1D directional channels was demonstrated to boost the photocurrent output and the photoconversion efficiency for solar water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEC water splitting
Carbon dots
ZnO nanorod arrays
Photosensitization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11574263, 11374253 and 51572128), the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics of Southeast University (No. I2015005), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province, and the Personnel Training Plan of Yangzhou University. And we thank the Testing Center of Yangzhou University for the Technical Supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image with size distribution (inset), (b) HRTEM image, (c) FT-IR and (d) C 1s XPS spectra of as-synthesized CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of CDs with (a, b) as-synthesized, (c, d) reduced and (e, f) passivated surfaces. The insets show the photo-absorption-induced charge transition models. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images with magnified views (insets) of (a) ZnO and (b) ZnO@CDs NRAs. (c) TEM, (d) HRTEM images and (e) EDS element mappings of single ZnO@CDs rod. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (b) linear sweep voltammetric
J − V
curves, (c) chopped-light amperometric
J − t
curves at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl, and (d) IPCE spectra with photoconversion efficiency curves (inset) of two contrastive ZnO and ZnO@CDs photoanodes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Amount of hydrogen evolution, and (b) corresponding photocurrent density versus time curves of ZnO@CDs photoanode at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl in 0.25 M Na2S and 0.35 M Na2SO3 electrolyte for four successive PEC operation cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic illustrations for water splitting, and photo-induced charge transition and transfer for PEC cell of CDs-sensitized ZnO NRAs photoanode. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Surface states engineering carbon dots as multi-band light active sensitizers for ZnO nanowire array photoanode to boost solar water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics Science and Technology, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronics & Nanomaterials, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient capture of solar photons is crucial in photo-electrochemical (PEC) water splitting devices for converting solar energy to hydrogen as an energy-dense carrier. Herein, we first reported the unique multi-band light absorption character on carbon dots (CDs) with controllable surface states and built an efficient photoanode by bonding such CDs as favorable solar photosensitizers on one-dimensional (1D) ZnO nanorod arrays (NRAs) for the PEC solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion. The multi-transition models related to surface CO, and COH states with different energy levels were identified to dominate the CDs' multi-model optical absorption covering the full-range visible region in the solar spectrum, which renders an excellent advantage of CDs in serving as the solar photosensitizer for photoelectric systems. Moreover, the fabricated ZnO@CDs heterostructure photoanode with the functionalized CDs used to harvest solar photons along with subsequent charge separation at heterointerface and transport along 1D directional channels was demonstrated to boost the photocurrent output and the photoconversion efficiency for solar water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial photosynthesis, which can drive the conversion of solar energy to chemical fuels, is considered as one of the most promising strategies for solar energy conversion and storage [1–4]. Solar-to-hydrogen (STH) conversion through photo-electrochemical (PEC) water splitting on semiconductor photoelectrodes has attracted widespread attention since Honda and Fujishima first explored the PEC water splitting on the TiO2 photoanode [5–12]. In addition to TiO2, ZnO materials with various nanostructures have emerged as promising photoanodes due to their low cost, easy preparation, nontoxicity and high electron mobility [13–16]. Especially, one dimensional (1D) ZnO nanorod arrays (NRAs) have been developed as an optimal architecture for PEC photoanodes based on their large surface area, long light-scattering pathway, 1D directional carrier transport, and low charge recombination loss [17,18]. However, ZnO with wide band gap of about 3.2 eV suffers from the limited response to ultraviolet (UV) light which composes less than 5% of the solar spectrum [19], imposing a fundamental restriction of the poor solar harvesting on the resultant STH efficiency. Therefore, extending the response spectrum of ZnO-based photoanodes to cover more of the visible and near-infrared (NIR) regions has been of great significance.

Many efforts have been taken to overcome the lack in sunlight response of ZnO photoelectrodes through several strategies, such as doping with hetero-ions [20], coupling with noble metal plasmon [21], and sensitizing with dye or narrow-band-gap quantum dots (QDs) [22–25]. Recently, earth-abundant and low-cost carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising photosensitizer in combination with semiconductor catalysts for solar hydrogen evolution because of their light-harvesting and electron transfer properties [26–30]. Noting that the inherent light absorption from the π-π* transition of little sp2 clusters in CDs was usually observed at UV or near visible regions [31,32], thus from this point, CDs are not enough suitable as visible-light active sensitizers. Interestingly, Kang and Liu et al. demonstrated the optical up-conversion properties of CDs and opened a seminal avenue for the design of visible- or even NIR-light responsive TiO2-based photoanodes by combining CDs as an up-converted-spectrum converter [33,34]. In view of the usually poor efficiency in up-conversion processes, it is strongly expected to cultivate more effective transition models in CDs for favoring solar photon harvesting in CDs-based solar conversion systems.

In studies on photoluminescence (PL) characters of CDs, the involvement of surface states in the photo-induced charge transition models has been widely accepted despite of the controversy on PL mechanism [35–37]. The surface states with a series of specific energy levels located below lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) have been thought to associated with the excitation-dependent PL behavior, implying the existence of photo-induced charge multi-transition models involved with surface states [38]. Such the multi-transition models behind the PL process are unfavorable for the emission quality and yield whereas can make the CDs' photoelectric response unlimited to the certain single- or two-photon absorption localized by the π-π* transition of sp2 carbons. On the basis of this knowledge, we speculate that the multi-transition models involved with surface states can be engineered to effectively broaden the working spectrum of CDs as photosensitizers in combination with wide band-gap ZnO PEC electrodes for solar water splitting.

Herein, we first described the photo-induced charge multi-transition models in CDs with the controllable surface states and its consequence of multi-model optical absorption. Then we built the unique CDs-sensitized ZnO NRAs (ZnO@CDs) photoanode for solar water splitting, which exhibited the significantly enhanced PEC activity relative to bare ZnO NRAs one. The laden CDs were demonstrated to function as an excellent solar photosensitizer to facilitate photon harvesting with subsequent charge production and separation, being responsible for the enhanced solar PEC activity.

Well-crystallized CDs with surface oxygenous groups were synthesized by the polymerization reaction between citric acid and urea under solvothermal condition similar to a previously reported method [37]. Typically, 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the reactor cooled naturally to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 16,000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. The precipitate was collected, washed thrice with ethanol by centrifugation at 16,000 rpm for 10 min to remove residual salts and alkali. Finally, the dissolution in deionized water produced a concentrated purple solution of CDs with the long-time stability in air at room temperature. For exploring the surface origins of optical properties, the surface-reduced and surface-passivated CDs were also prepared via a reduction treatment of introducing sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 0.05 g) as reducing agent into the 20 mL of 1 mg mL−1 CDs solution with magnetic stirring overnight at room temperature and a hydrothermal reaction from the citric acid (1 g) and urea (0.5 g) at 160 °C for 6 h. We found that the different solvents could induce difference in their reactions to form CDs from the polymerization reaction between citric acid and urea. The DMF solvent tends to form the nitrogen-rich CDs with abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on surface; the aqueous solvent tends to form the nitrogen-rich CDs conjugated with amino-groups on surface.

The CDs-sensitized ZnO NRAs photoelectrode was fabricated according to the preparation process shown in Scheme S1. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glasses (1 cm × 2 cm) were washed successively with acetone, ethanol and deionized water by ultrasonication, and then dried with N2 stream at room temperature. The clear FTO substrate was firstly immersed twice into the 0.005 M ethanol solution of Zn(CH3COO)2 for about 10 s and dried at 60 °C, then annealed at 350 °C for 30 min to form ZnO dense seed layer on the conducting side. The FTO substrate with seed layer was placed vertically into the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclaves filled with 20 mL of equimolar Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and C6H12N4 (0.05 M) aqueous solution and kept at 95 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the deposited substrate was taken out of solution, and carefully rinsed with deionized water and ethanol. Well-aligned ZnO NRAs grown on FIO substrate were used as the scaffold to anchor the CDs by spin-coating method. The 20 mL of aqueous solution containing 20 mg of as-synthesized CDs was prepared as the spin-coating sol. Spin coating in five times ensures the CDs uniformly deposited on the entire surface of the ZnO nanorods. Finally, the ZnO@CDs composite NRAs were annealed at 150 °C in N2 stream for 30 min to improve the ZnO crystallization and conjunction with CDs.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were investigated by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi, S-4800II), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F30, FE), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) equipped with X-ray energy dispersive spectrum (EDS). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Varian Cary 670 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source operating at 150 W, and the binding energy was calibrated according to the C 1s peak at 284.6 eV. UV–Vis absorption spectra were drawn by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 5000) in the range from 200 to 800 nm. The PL spectra were recorded at room temperature with a luminescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi FL4600) using Xe lamp emission with regulatory wavelength as excitation source.

The potentials were measured vs. Ag/AgCl electrode and reported vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by the transformation calculation using a Nernst conversion equation [21].
(1)

+ 0.059 pH

E

is the standard potential of Ag/AgCl reference electrode (0.1976 V vs. RHE at 25 °C),
E
RHE
and
E
Ag/AgCl
are the converted potential vs. RHE and the measured potential vs. Ag/AgCl.

The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiencies (IPCE) were evaluated based on the measured photocurrent densities with several single band-pass filters of λ ± 15 nm for 400, 420, 435, 450, 475, 500, 520, 550, 650 and 700 nm through the following derivation [39].
(2)
IPCE
( % )
=
n
(
electron
)
N
(
photon
)
=
(
J × t × S
)
/ e
(
P
light
× S × t
)
/
h ν
=
h × c × J
P
light
× λ × e
=
1239.8
(
nm · V
)
× J
(
mA ·
cm
− 2
)
P
light
(
mW ·
cm
− 2
)
× λ
(
nm
)
× 100
where 1239.8 represents a multiplication of Planck's constant ( h ) and the light speed ( c ), n is the quantity of electrons, N is the number of photons, J is the photocurrent density, λ is the incident light wavelength, and
P
light
is the irradiation power density, respectively. The photoconversion efficiencies ( η ) were estimated by the following equation [1].
(3)
η =
J
(
1.23 −
V
app
)
P
light
where
V
app
is the applied potential vs. RHE, J is the measured photocurrent density at
V
app
, and
P
light
is the power density of the incident light.

In our experiments, the CDs were prepared from polymerization reaction between citric acid and urea in DMF solvent. Fig. 1 a shows the TEM image of as-synthesized CDs, in which the CDs can be seen to be well dispersed and have a narrow size distribution with the most probable size of 4.0 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). The HRTEM image in Fig. 1b shows the CDs are little and circular graphitic crystals with lattice spacing of around 0.21 nm, which can be indexed to the {1
1 ¯
00} planes of graphite [40]. The surface chemical composition of CDs was detected by the FT-IR and XPS spectrometers. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1c) mainly displays the stretching vibrations of COH (～3446 cm−1), CO (～1711 cm−1), CC (～1665 cm−1) and NH (～1587 cm−1) [41]. The full-range XPS and EDS spectra reveal the chemical composition of C, N, and O elements (Fig. S1). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows four de-convoluted peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 287.8 and 288.8 eV, which are ascribed to graphitic CC/CC, COH, CN and CO groups, respectively [42]. These FT-IR and XPS data indicate the fact that there are a large number of hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the CDs' surface. The surface bonding with some specific groups is favorable to make CDs more hydrophilic and easy to attach onto the semiconductor surfaces. More importantly, these surface groups were found to have significant influence on the optical absorption and the subsequent charge transition in the CDs.

In order to identify the electronic structure of CDs with rich surface groups, the photo-induced charge transition properties were investigated in detail based on the analyses for the UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra. Fig. 2 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CDs, presenting the broad and strong absorption consisting of three absorption bands over the UV and visible regions. The typical absorption band located at 343 nm can be attributed to π–π* orbital transition in sp2 domains [43]. The other two broad absorption bands centered at 420 and 530 nm can be assigned to the transition models involved with surface states at different energy levels. Obviously, the multi-band light absorption character can endow CDs with greater potential of harvesting solar photons for photovoltaic or photocatalytic systems. Fig. 2b shows the PL spectra, in which the emissions clearly shift toward longer wavelengths with several typical peaks as the excitation wavelengths increase. This excitation-dependent PL behavior implies that the multi-transition models involved with different states occur selectively in different probabilities at different excitation wavelengths. In view of the identification for surface chemical groups, the surface oxidized groups like CO, CO and OCOH can be reasonably assumed to introduce the surface states with different energies between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and LUMO of CDs and consequently to dominate the multiple-band optical absorption and the excitation-dependent PL emission in the present CDs [44]. In order to further clarify the origin on surface states behind optical properties, the UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of CDs with reduced and passivated surfaces are depicted in Fig. 2c–f to compare with those of as-synthesized CDs. For the surface-reduced CDs with NaBH4, the signals of CO groups in the FT-IR and XPS spectra are hardly observed whereas the signal of COH group increases compared to as-synthesized CDs (Fig. S2). This indicates the transform of carbonyl to hydroxyl groups after the reduction treatment. Interestingly, the optical absorption band at 420 nm nearly disappears and one at 530 nm grows relatively to the normalized π–π* transition absorption in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of reduced CDs (Fig. 2c), and meanwhile the PL spectrum shows the less excitation-dependent emission peak position located at around 550 nm in addition to the π–π* recombination emission at 465 nm. Hence, besides the π–π* transition from conjugated sp2 carbons, notably, two transition models related with surface CO and COH states are identified to respectively determine the absorption bands at 420 and 530 nm, and moreover the surface CO state may contain a series of energy levels to cause the PL peak position depending on excitation energy, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. The surface-reduced CDs manipulate the avoiding of the CO state in the photo-induced charge transition models (inset in Fig. 2c), leading to the decline of certain absorption band at 420 nm and the weaken dependence of PL peak position within 500–550 nm on excitation wavelength. For the surface-passivated CDs with amino groups, the most of surface bond signals come from the nitrous carbons rather than the oxygenous carbons (Fig. S2), which supports the successful removal of surface oxidized states. Accordingly, the multi-model absorption turns into a dominant and symmetric UV absorption with a weak shoulder at around 640 nm related with the two-photon absorption. As the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 700 nm, the PL spectra show both the down- and up-conversion PL behaviors, whereas their emission peak position locked at 440 nm almost remains unchanged. The monotonous absorption and PL features imply that only a single π–π* model transition takes place along with the multiphoton active process excluding the involvement of surface states after the passivation treatment (inset of Fig. 2e). Furthermore, the air annealing at 150 °C was further used to remove amino groups and reconstruct CDs' surface with oxygenous groups, leading to the return of multi-model absorption character (Fig. S3). Herein, the photo-induced charge multi-transition models related to CC, CO, and COH electronic states with different energy levels were identified to dominate the multi-band light absorption character, which is meaningful insight into developing capacity of CDs in harvesting photons for solar conversion applications.

To examine the function of CDs in serving as a solar photosensitizer, the vertically oriented ZnO NRAs decorated with CDs were fabricated on FTO via hydrothermal growth of ZnO followed by spin coating of CDs and used as a PEC photoanode for solar water splitting. The ZnO NRAs can only response to the UV light while can directionally transport electrons with high mobility and minor recombination loss; the laden CDs are expected to harvest solar photons in the visible band and produce more ‘hot’ electrons for transfer to the adjacent ZnO. The SEM images with a cross-sectional view in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S4 show the growth of dense and vertically aligned ZnO NRAs with uniform rod length of ～2 μm on FTO glass. The amplified SEM image in the inset reveals that ZnO nanorods are of hexagonal shape with average diameter of about 180 nm. Notably, the vertically oriented NRAs are very beneficial to both confine the incident light and transfer photo-excited carriers via abundant 1D channels, being a favorable 3D architecture for many photoelectric devices [45]. After decorating with CDs, the dense alignment of vertical oriented NRAs remains almost unchanged, as shown in Fig. 3b, whereas the NRs transform from hexagonal into cylindrical shapes owing to the CDs' loading on ZnO surface in spite of the indiscernible trace of CDs smaller than the SEM resolution limit. The TEM image of a typical single ZnO@CDs rod shown in Fig. 3c further reveals the hierarchical surface with CDs loading layer of around 5 nm. The HRTEM image at heterointerface (Fig. 3d) presents two groups of lattice fringes with different spacings of 0.21 and 0.26 nm, which can be ascribed to the {1
1 ¯
00} plane of CDs and the {0002} plane of hexagonal ZnO, respectively. The XPS characterization (Fig. S5) for ZnO@CDs NRAs further determines the combination of ZnO and CDs, and moreover the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s profiles reveal the remains of CO and COH groups. These specific groups on CDs are favorable to link with ZnO nanorods and especially can introduce a series of surface states on CDs to steer the multi-model charge transitions in favor for visible light absorption and subsequent photo-induced charge process. In addition, the EDX mappings of Zn, O and C elements in a single rod show their uniform distributions in the rod-shaped block (Fig. 3e). These observations verify that the CDs were uniformly deposited on the sidewall of ZnO to form a thin outer shell. Moreover, the intimate hetero-shell-core construction can offer the structural guarantee for charge transfer and synergistic action.

As the proof-of-concept application, the solar water splitting were performed in the three-electrode system to investigate the PEC properties of the pristine ZnO NRAs and the composited ZnO@CDs photoanode with a Pt-wire as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, respectively. The optical absorption abilities of two electrodes are compared by their UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 4 a. The pristine ZnO NRAs display a sharp absorption edge with a cutoff wavelength below around 383 nm because of their wide band gap (～3.23 eV). After the CDs loading on ZnO NRAs, the extended light absorption occurs in the visible band, which is associated to the photo-induced charge multi-model transitions in CDs with surface states. Thus the ZnO@CDs heterostructure electrode is endowed with the desired capacity in harvesting solar photons by the integration of CDs with the unique multi-band light absorption. Fig. 4b shows the
J − V
curves of ZnO and ZnO@CDs NRAs photoanodes in the dark and under the simulated sunlight illumination. The dark scans for two electrodes only exhibit a weak background current density at μA cm−2 scale. While under illumination, their photocurrent densities continuously increase after the external potentials exceed the onset potentials, corresponding to the production and transport of photo-generated charge carriers in the photoanodes. Importantly, the ZnO@CDs photoanode exhibits the smaller onset potential of 0.3 V vs. RHE, relative to the bare ZnO counterpart (0.45 V vs. RHE), and yields the larger photocurrent output in the potential window. The featured current density of the ZnO@CDs photoanode at an external potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE shows an enhancement of 210% compared to that of the ZnO one. Fig. 4c shows the
J − t
curves under the chopped-light illumination at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. Both of two electrodes exhibit good switching behavior, and the ZnO@CDs photoanode presents the two-fold increase in photocurrent ON/OFF over the bare ZnO one. The growth of photocurrent output and the negative shift of onset potential in the ZnO@CDs photoanode can be attributed to the CDs-expanded light absorption, which also provide a convincing proof for the transfer of electrons excited in CDs toward ZnO. The IPCE curves of bare ZnO and ZnO@CDs photoanodes in the visible region are shown in Fig. 4d, which coincide with their optical absorption features in Fig. 4a, indicating the PEC reaction proceeds through the charge transition models induced by photoabsorption in the photoanodes. The enhanced IPCE values of the ZnO@CDs photoanode in the visible region further confirm that the above conclusion that the enhanced light harvesting of CDs can effectively contribute to the photocurrent output. Furthermore, the photoconversion efficiencies of two photoanodes for PEC water splitting were evaluated and compared in the inset of Fig. 4d. The maximum photoconversion efficiency of the ZnO@CDs photoanode was 0.36% at 1.0 V vs. RHE, which is over 6 times larger than the ZnO one. These PEC results convincingly demonstrate the benefit from CDs acting as photosensitizers in the ZnO@CDs composited photoanode.

The hydrogen evolved from the water splitting over the ZnO@CDs photoanode was detected by the gas chromatography with successive PEC operation cycles in 0.25 M Na2S and 0.35 M Na2SO3 electrolyte solution. The ZnO@CDs photoanode was illuminated from the front side under the simulated sunlight illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2), the hydrogen evolved at the Pt cathode was collected with an applied potential of 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Fig. 5 a shows the hydrogen generation amount as a function of time in four testing cycles for 4 h. The linear increase of hydrogen production amount in four cycles reveals an average rate of ～250 μmol h−1 cm−1, which is comparable with or higher than recently reported values of ZnO-based photoanodes for PEC water splitting [46–48]. In any operation cycles, the amount of evolved hydrogen is proportional to the irradiation time and meanwhile the detected photocurrent density nearly remains stable. This indicates that the observed photocurrent corresponds to the hydrogen evolution reaction, supporting the excellent corrosion resistance of ZnO@CDs photoanode in the PEC process.

The energy band alignment is proposed in Fig. 6 to understand the photo-induced charge transition and transfer routes. First, the multi-band light absorption and excitation-dependent PL properties of CDs identify the existence of surface states with a series of specific energy levels below LUMO and its consequence of photo-induced charge multi-model transitions in CDs. Second, the CDs-improved photocurrent response even at the potential of 0 V or in the visible band reveals the spontaneous injection of electrons excited in CDs to ZnO, thus the flat band potential of ZnO should correspond to the minimum energy required for the electrons in LUMO with surface states of CDs to inject into the conduction band (CB) of ZnO. Third, the positive IPCE values observed in the visible band for ZnO@CDs photoanode without any hole sacrificial agents, indicating that the holes left in HOMO of CDs can drive water oxidization to complete the whole redox cycle, so the HOMO level of CDs should be below the oxidation level into O2 for water. In addition, the CDs were analyzed using linear potential scans to estimate the HOMO and LUMO levels at approximately −0.7 and 1.85 eV vs. RHE, respectively (Fig. S6) [40]. The band-gap energy of the π–π* transition determined from the potential scans was 2.55 eV, in agreement with that obtained via the converted curve of
(
α h ν
)
2
versus
h ν
from the optical absorption spectrum (Fig. S7a). The energy levels of surface states were estimated in range of −0.12～-0.56 eV vs. RHE based on the multi-band absorption edges. The CB bottom and valence band (VB) top levels of ZnO were considered at −0.45 and 2.81 eV vs. RHE at pH value of 7.0 [13], with a wide band gap of around 3.26 eV corresponding to its optical absorption edge (Fig. S7b). Therefore, the sensitization mechanism of CDs with surface states is suggested for the enhanced PEC activity of ZnO@CDs. As shown in Fig. 6, the equipped surface states below LUMO in CDs can introduce more transition models (k2, k3 and k4) besides the π–π* transition (k1) to enhance the light-harvesting capacity especially in the visible band. The excited electrons into the LUMO and part of surface states transfer to the CB of ZnO in contact (k5) and then fast transport along the axial direction of ZnO NRs to the FTO substrate linked with the external circuit. Notably, the photon harvesting and electron output can be effectively boosted due to the involvement of surface states in charge transitions although some excited electrons in the surface states below the ZnO CB cannot directly inject into ZnO. In addition to the photosensitization action, the type-Ⅱ band alignment at ZnO@CDs interface steers the reverse transport of electrons and holes (k5 and k6), further facilitating the separation of electron-hole pairs before their recombination.

In summary, the inexpensive CDs synthesized via a solvothermal route were equipped with rich surface states and exhibited the unique multi-band light absorption character due to the photo-induced charge multi-model transitions. The CDs with enhanced photon-harvesting ability were designed as solar photosensitizers in combination with ZnO NRAs photoanode to extend the spectrum response range and improve photoconversion efficiency for PEC water splitting. The type-Ⅱ band alignment matched at heterointerface steers the reverse transfer and rapid separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The over six-fold increase in photoconversion efficiency was achieved in the ZnO@CDs photoanode with the superior and stable hydrogen evolution rate of 250 μmol h−1 cm−1 at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl, compared to pure ZnO one. Our findings show a promising avenue of using surface-functionalized CDs to design the full-spectrum visible light active systems for solar conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 11574263, 11374253 and 51572128), the Open Research Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics of Southeast University (No. I2015005), the Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province, and the Personnel Training Plan of Yangzhou University. And we thank the Testing Center of Yangzhou University for the Technical Supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.05.095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ti3C2 MXene co-catalyst on metal sulfide photo-absorbers for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic hydrogen production
Toyota MIRAI fuel cell vehicle and progress toward a future hydrogen society

Hematite films decorated with nanostructured ferric oxyhydroxide as photoanodes for efficient and stable photoelectrochemical water splitting
